name: Franklin Knight Lane
summary: U.S. Secretary of the Interior (1913–1920); cited in GPB among American officials of the period, an incidental name.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a U.S. cabinet official, no direct Bahá'í role.

name: Franklin MacVeagh
summary: U.S. Secretary of the Treasury (1909–1913); named once in GPB as a recipient of attention during the period, incidental.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention of a U.S. cabinet official with no Bahá'í role.

name: Furúg̱híyyih (half-sister of Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí)
summary: Half-sister of the arch-Covenant-breaker Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí; her death from cancer is noted in GPB among the afflictions visited upon the Covenant-breaking family.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a Covenant-breaker family member, no independent role.

name: Fáṭimatu'z-Zahrá bint Muḥammad
aliases: the daughter of the Apostle of God
summary: Daughter of the Prophet Muḥammad, wife of Imám ‘Alí and mother of the Imáms Ḥasan and Ḥusayn; the most revered woman in Shí'ih Islam. Shoghi Effendi ranks her among Sarah, Ásíyih and the Virgin Mary, and notes Bahá'u'lláh's lineal descent from her and Ṭáhirih's naming after her.
importance_reason: Foremost woman of Islam, Bahá'u'lláh's ancestress and the type for Ṭáhirih; taught for her station though not a Bahá'í figure.
characterizations: Wikipedia: Fatima bint Muhammad was the youngest daughter of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and his wife Khadija, and one of the most important figures in Shia Islam.

name: Fáṭimih Baraghání, Ṭáhirih
summary: Foremost woman of the Bábí Dispensation and the only female among the Báb's eighteen Letters of the Living; a poetess of Qazvín who unveiled herself at the Conference of Badasht to proclaim the new Dispensation, and was martyred in 1852. Surnamed Ṭáhirih (the Pure One) by Bahá'u'lláh and Qurratu’l-‘Ayn (Solace of the Eyes) by Siyyid Káẓim.
importance_reason: A Letter of the Living and the supreme heroine of the Faith, type of women's emancipation, with 119 mentions across DB and GPB.
characterizations: Wikipedia: In 1844 she became the seventeenth disciple or 'Letter of the Living' of the Báb. As the only woman in this initial group of disciples, she is often compared to Mary Magdalene.
Bahai-library (GPB): immortalized as Ṭáhirih (the Pure One) by the 'Tongue of Glory,' and surnamed Qurratu’l-‘Ayn (Solace of the Eyes) by Siyyid Káẓim.

name: Fáṭimih-Bagum
summary: A courageous Bahá'í woman of Mans̱hád who was brutally tortured and martyred; remembered in GPB as a victim of anti-Bahá'í persecution.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a martyr with a vivid but isolated account.

name: Gabriel
summary: The archangel of revelation in the Abrahamic traditions who conveyed the Qur'án to Muḥammad; in GPB invoked as a symbol of divine inspiration across the Dispensations.
importance_reason: Recurring scriptural/angelic figure invoked symbolically rather than a historical Bahá'í participant.

name: George Grey Barnard
summary: Prominent American sculptor whose praise of the Bahá'í House of Worship design ('the greatest creation since the Gothic period') is quoted in GPB.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a non-Bahá'í who praised the Mashriqu'l-Adhkár.

name: George Nathaniel Curzon, 1st Marquess Curzon of Kedleston
summary: British statesman, Viceroy of India and Foreign Secretary, author of 'Persia and the Persian Question' (1892); repeatedly quoted in GPB for his admiring testimony to Bábí heroism, especially that of Ṭáhirih.
importance_reason: 46 mentions but functions chiefly as a Western witness quoted for testimony to Bábí history, not an actor in it.
characterizations: Wikipedia: George Nathaniel Curzon, 1st Marquess Curzon of Kedleston, was a British Conservative statesman who served as Viceroy of India and as Foreign Secretary.

name: Gul-Muḥammad-i-Ardikání
summary: Aged Bahá'í of Ardikán savagely beaten and killed by a mob; remembered in GPB as a martyr of the later persecutions.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a martyr with a vivid but isolated account.

name: Gurgín Khán
summary: Nephew of Manúc̱hihr Khán (the Mu‘tamid), governor of Iṣfahán; after his uncle's death he destroyed the will, seized the property, and betrayed the Báb's whereabouts, inducing the Sháh to summon the Báb to Ṭihrán.
importance_reason: Recurring antagonist (11 mentions) in the Báb's Iṣfahán episode whose treachery advanced events.
characterizations: Bahai-library (DB): After Manúchihr Khán's death his nephew Gurgín Khán discovered and destroyed his will, seized his property, and informed against the Báb's residence.

name: Gustav Stresemann
summary: German Foreign Minister and Nobel Peace laureate; named once in GPB as a recipient of Bahá'í literature presented by Martha Root.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a statesman contacted by Martha Root.

name: Gyula Germanus
summary: Hungarian orientalist (later a Muslim convert) who led a deputation of Young Turks welcoming ‘Abdu’l-Bahá; an incidental GPB mention.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as host of a welcoming deputation.

name: G̱hulám ‘Alí, son of Ḥusayn-i-‘Arab (father of Nabíl)
summary: Father of Nabíl-i-A‘ẓam (the chronicler of The Dawn-Breakers), of the nomadic Ṭáhirí tribe of Khurásán; mentioned only in Nabíl's autobiographical note.
importance_reason: Incidental DB mention as the chronicler's father, no independent role.

name: G̱hulám-Riḍá
summary: A Bábí described in DB as a man of exceptional courage; a single-mention episode participant.
importance_reason: One-mention DB name with only a brief characterization.

name: G̱hulám-Riḍáy-i-Kúc̱hik
summary: A Bábí marksman noted in DB for his skill; a single-mention episode participant.
importance_reason: One-mention DB name distinguished only by marksmanship.

name: G̱hulám-Riḍáy-i-Yazdí (captain of the forces)
summary: Bábí captain at Nayríz appointed by Vaḥíd to lead sorties against the besiegers; a minor defender in the Nayríz episode.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions as a Nayríz fort captain, a minor episode participant.

name: G̱hulám-Ḥusayn-i-S̱hus̱htarí
summary: A merchant who guided the narrator to Mírzá Aḥmad's house; an incidental DB mention.
importance_reason: One-mention DB name serving only as a guide in a passing scene.

name: G̱hází ibn Fayṣal (King G̱hází I of ‘Iráq)
summary: King of Iraq (r. 1933–1939), son of Faysal I; named in GPB as recipient of messages from the Iraq Bahá'í Assembly on his accession and marriage.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a sovereign corresponded with by a Bahá'í Assembly.

name: Haggai the Prophet
summary: Post-exilic Hebrew prophet (c. 520 BC); cited in GPB for his prophecy of the 'Desire of all nations,' applied to Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a prophet whose words are read as foretelling Bahá'u'lláh.

name: Harlan Foster Ober
summary: Early American Bahá'í who, with Hooper Harris, undertook the first Bahá'í teaching journey to India and Burma (1907); a pioneer of the Faith in the East.
importance_reason: Pioneer teacher of an early Eastern travel mission, taught as part of the Faith's early American history.

name: Harold Van Buren Magonigle
summary: American architect and President of the Architectural League who praised the Bahá'í Temple design as 'the first new idea in architecture since the 13th century'; an incidental GPB mention.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a non-Bahá'í who praised the Mashriqu'l-Adhkár.

name: Hayát-Qulí Khán
summary: Governor of Karand, of the ‘Alíyu’lláhí sect, who warmly received Bahá'u'lláh during His journey to ‘Iráq; an incidental GPB mention.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a local governor who showed Bahá'u'lláh hospitality.

name: Helen Smith Goodall
summary: Early California Bahá'í named in GPB among those associated with the first stirrings of the Faith in North America.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention among early North American believers.

name: Henry Lewis Stimson
summary: U.S. Secretary of State (and later War); named in GPB only as the signatory of a 1929 State Department certificate.
importance_reason: Single incidental GPB mention as a document signatory.

name: Henry Martyn
summary: Anglican missionary and Persian Bible translator who worked in Shíráz; cited once in DB in a passing comparison about conversions in Persia.
importance_reason: Single DB mention in an incidental aside.

name: Herbert Louis Samuel, 1st Viscount Samuel
summary: First British High Commissioner for Palestine (1920–1925) who paid calls on ‘Abdu’l-Bahá and attended His funeral; remembered in GPB for his official respect toward the Master.
importance_reason: Six mentions as a high official whose interactions with ‘Abdu’l-Bahá are recurringly noted.

name: Hippolyte Dreyfus-Barney
summary: The first Frenchman to embrace the Bahá'í Faith; through his translations, writings and travels he established and consolidated the Faith in France.
importance_reason: First French Bahá'í and notable scholar-translator, a taught figure of early European Bahá'í history.

name: Hon. William Sulzer
summary: American politician, Governor of New York; named once in GPB in a list of dignitaries, incidental.
importance_reason: Single bare GPB mention among public figures.

name: Hooper C. Harris
summary: Early American Bahá'í who, with Harlan Ober, made the first Bahá'í teaching journey through India and Burma (1907).
importance_reason: Pioneer teacher of an early Eastern travel mission, taught in early American Bahá'í history.

name: Horatio Herbert Kitchener
summary: British field marshal and Consul-General in Egypt; named once in GPB as someone to whom ‘Abdu’l-Bahá was introduced, incidental.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a dignitary introduced to ‘Abdu’l-Bahá.

name: Hosea
summary: Hebrew prophet of the 8th century BC; cited once in GPB for the phrase 'a door of hope' applied prophetically.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention of a prophet quoted for a prophetic phrase.

name: Howard C. Struven
summary: Early American Bahá'í who, with Mason Remey, made the first Bahá'í circumnavigation of the globe; a pioneer traveler of the Faith.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention but noted for the first Bahá'í round-the-world journey.

name: Howard MacNutt
summary: Early American Bahá'í, compiler of 'The Promulgation of Universal Peace'; named in GPB among those associated with the first stirrings of the Faith in North America.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention but a notable early American believer and compiler.

name: Huda Sha‘ráví
summary: Pioneering Egyptian feminist leader; named once in GPB as a figure of the period, incidental.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention identifying her only as the Egyptian feminist leader.

name: Hádí (infant son of Ḥujjat)
summary: Infant son of Ḥujjat-i-Zanjání who died of burns during the Zanján upheaval; an incidental but poignant DB mention.
importance_reason: Single DB mention as a child victim in the Zanján episode.

name: Hájí Muḥammad Taqí (registrar of the fort, Nayríz)
summary: Registrar of the Bábí fort at Nayríz; a single-mention minor figure of that episode.
importance_reason: One-mention DB name with only a role label.

name: Hárúnu’r-Ras̱híd, the ‘Abbáside Ḵhalífih
summary: ‘Abbásid Caliph (r. 786–809); cited in DB as the one who ordered the poisoning of the seventh Imám, Músá-Káẓim.
importance_reason: Single DB mention; a famous caliph but appearing only as the poisoner of an Imám.

name: Húlágú Khán
summary: Mongol conqueror who sacked Baghdad in 1258 and ended the ‘Abbásid Caliphate; cited once in DB as a historical date-marker.
importance_reason: Single DB mention used only to anchor a historical date.

name: Ibn-i-Muljam
summary: The Khárijite assassin of Imám ‘Alí at Kúfih (A.H. 40); a single DB mention in a historical note.
importance_reason: One DB mention as the assassin of Imám ‘Alí in a footnote-style note.

name: Ibn-i-Sa‘d
summary: Umayyad commander at Karbalá associated with the slaying of Imám Ḥusayn; cited once in DB in a rhetorical allusion.
importance_reason: One DB mention only in a quoted rhetorical phrase.

name: Ibn-i-Zíyád
summary: Umayyad governor responsible for sending forces against Imám Ḥusayn at Karbalá; cited once in DB in a rhetorical allusion.
importance_reason: One DB mention only in a quoted rhetorical phrase.

name: Ibráhím Pás̱há of Egypt
summary: Egyptian military commander; referenced once in DB only to date a man's earlier military service.
importance_reason: One DB mention used only as a background reference.

name: Ignác Goldziher
summary: World-renowned Hungarian orientalist; named once in GPB as a scholar of standing, incidental.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention identifying him only as a famous orientalist.

name: Ilyás ‘Abbúd
summary: Christian neighbor of Bahá'u'lláh in ‘Akká whose adjoining house was later joined to the House of ‘Abbúd, where the Kitáb-i-Aqdas was revealed; initially wary of his Prisoner Neighbor.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention, but his name attaches to the historically significant House of ‘Abbúd.

name: Imám Ja‘far-i-Ṣádiq
summary: The sixth of the Twelve Shí'ih Imáms, a revered teacher of jurisprudence and tradition; his sayings are repeatedly quoted in DB, and his prophecies were venerated by figures such as Siyyid Káẓim.
importance_reason: Five DB mentions; a major Shí'ih Imám frequently quoted, though appearing in DB chiefly through his traditions.
characterizations: Wikipedia: Ja'far al-Sadiq was the sixth Imam of Twelver and Isma'ili Shia Muslims and a major figure in Islamic jurisprudence.

name: Imám Muḥammad al-Javád
summary: The ninth Shí'ih Imám; cited once in DB in connection with a Shí'ih tradition of miraculous travel.
importance_reason: Single DB mention of a Shí'ih Imám in a traditional anecdote.

name: Imám Muḥammad-Báqir ibn ‘Alí
summary: The fifth Shí'ih Imám; cited once in DB's genealogical/martyrology note of the poisoned Imáms.
importance_reason: Single DB mention within the list of martyred Imáms.

name: Imám Muḥammad-Taqí ibn ‘Alí-Riḍá
summary: The ninth Shí'ih Imám (Muḥammad-Taqí, son of Imám Riḍá); cited once in DB's note of the poisoned Imáms.
importance_reason: Single DB mention within the list of martyred Imáms.

name: Imám Músá Káẓim
summary: The seventh of the Twelve Shí'ih Imáms, poisoned under Hárúnu’r-Ras̱híd; referenced in DB as ancestor of the Ṣafaví kings and through related shrine and lineage notes.
importance_reason: Five DB mentions of a major Shí'ih Imám, appearing largely in genealogical and shrine references.

name: Imám Ḥasan ibn ‘Alí
summary: The second Shí'ih Imám, elder son of ‘Alí and Fáṭimih, poisoned c. A.H. 50; cited in DB's note of the martyred Imáms.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions of a major Shí'ih Imám, appearing in the martyrology note.

name: Imám Ḥasan-i-‘Askarí ibn ‘Alí-Naqí
summary: The eleventh Shí'ih Imám and father of the awaited Qá'im (the Twelfth Imám); cited in DB's note of the poisoned Imáms.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions of a major Shí'ih Imám, appearing in the martyrology note.

name: Imám Ḥusayn ibn 'Alí
summary: The third Shí'ih Imám, grandson of the Prophet, martyred at Karbalá in 680 CE and revered throughout Shí'ih Islam as the Chief of Martyrs; in Bahá'í scripture His martyrdom and 'return' are linked to the advent of the Qá'im.
importance_reason: 65 mentions across DB and GPB; the supreme Shí'ih martyr whose example and prophesied return frame the Bábí advent.
characterizations: Wikipedia: Husayn ibn Ali was a grandson of the Islamic prophet Muhammad and the third Shia imam, whose martyrdom at the Battle of Karbala is a central event in Shia Islam.

name: Imám ‘Alí Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín ibn Ḥusayn
summary: The fourth Shí'ih Imám, son of Imám Ḥusayn and the Sásánid princess Shahribánú; cited in DB's note of the poisoned Imáms.
importance_reason: Single DB mention of a major Shí'ih Imám in the martyrology note.

name: Imám ‘Alí-Naqí ibn Muḥammad-Taqí
summary: The tenth Shí'ih Imám; cited once in DB's note of the poisoned Imáms.
importance_reason: Single DB mention of a Shí'ih Imám in the martyrology note.

name: Imám ‘Alí-ibn-i-Músá’r-Riḍá (Imám Riḍá)
summary: The eighth of the Twelve Shí'ih Imáms, poisoned near Ṭús and buried at Mas̱hhad (which takes its sanctity from him); referenced repeatedly in DB through the great shrine and pilgrimage at Mas̱hhad.
importance_reason: Eight DB mentions of a major Shí'ih Imám, prominent through the Mas̱hhad shrine that anchors several scenes.

name: Imám-Zádih Ḥamzih
summary: Descendant (son) of the seventh Imám Músá-Káẓim whose tomb is noted in DB as a local shrine; an incidental topographical reference.
importance_reason: Single DB mention as the namesake of a local shrine.

name: Isabella Davis Brittingham
summary: Early American Bahá'í teacher named in GPB among those associated with the first stirrings of the Faith in North America.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention among early North American believers.

name: Isaiah the Prophet
summary: Greatest of the Hebrew prophets (8th century BC); cited repeatedly in GPB for prophecies ('Glory of the Lord,' 'Prince of Peace,' the Branch from the stem of Jesse) read as foretelling Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: Seven GPB mentions; a major scriptural prophet quoted for messianic prophecy, not a Bahá'í actor.

name: Isfandíyár
summary: A trusted servant in Bahá'u'lláh's household, remembered for his fidelity; cited once in DB in a domestic scene.
importance_reason: Single DB mention as a household servant noted for faithfulness.

name: Iskandar-i-Zanjání
summary: A Bábí of Zanján who bore Mullá Ḥusayn's body to the fort on the night of his martyrdom and laid it at Quddús's feet; a minor but memorable episode figure.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions tied to a memorable act at the fort of Shaykh Ṭabarsí.

name: Ismu’lláhu’l-Asad
summary: A Bábí to whom Nabíl credits help in collecting martyr names for DB; an incidental source-acknowledgment.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions only as a source Nabíl thanks.

name: Ismu’lláhu’l-Javád
summary: A Bábí to whom Nabíl credits help in collecting martyr names for DB; an incidental source-acknowledgment.
importance_reason: Single DB mention only as a source Nabíl thanks.

name: Ismu’lláhu’l-Mím
summary: A Bábí to whom Nabíl credits help in collecting martyr names for DB; an incidental source-acknowledgment.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions only as a source Nabíl thanks.

name: Ismá‘íl Áqá
summary: ‘Abdu’l-Bahá's faithful gardener in the Holy Land, whom the Master would bid pluck flowers for friends and enemies alike; a beloved minor figure.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as the Master's gardener in a tender anecdote.

name: Ismá‘íl-i-Zanjání
summary: A Bábí of Zanján listed among the participants of that upheaval; a minor episode name.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions as a Zanján participant with little detail.

name: Iṭá-Bábá-i-Mázindarání
summary: A martyr of Mázindarán listed in DB's roster of the fallen; a one-mention martyr-list entry.
importance_reason: One-mention DB martyr-list entry.

name: J. W. Jack
summary: An Oxford academic named once in GPB; an incidental reference.
importance_reason: Single bare GPB mention of an academic.

name: Ja'far-Qulí Khán
summary: Brother of the Grand Vizír who acted as Bahá'u'lláh's host on the journey toward ‘Iráq and urged Him to remain concealed; a recurring background figure of that period.
importance_reason: 13 mentions as Bahá'u'lláh's host at a pivotal juncture, though a secondary figure.
characterizations: Bahai-library (GPB): the Grand Vizír's brother, Ja‘far-Qulí Khán, who was acting as His host.

name: Jacob
summary: Hebrew patriarch (Israel); invoked once in GPB through Bahá'u'lláh's words comparing Jacob's grief to His own sorrows.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention used in a scriptural allusion.

name: Jalál the Nayrízí, called Bulbul
summary: A Nayrízí, nicknamed 'Bulbul' (nightingale) by Na‘ím, who betrayed his fellow-citizens by revealing their names; a single-mention informer.
importance_reason: One DB mention as a local informer in the Nayríz persecutions.

name: Jalíl-i-Tabrízí
summary: One of Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí's henchmen who espoused the Covenant-breakers' cause; a minor antagonist of the ‘Akká period.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention as a named Covenant-breaker henchman.

name: James Baillie Fraser
summary: Scottish travel-writer who journeyed through Persia (1830s–40s); quoted in DB for his observations on the misrule of the Qájár princes.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions as a Western traveler quoted for background on Persia.

name: James Darmesteter
summary: French orientalist quoted in GPB/DB for his testimony that the Bábí Faith spread across Persia and held the seeds of the country's regeneration.
importance_reason: Two mentions as a Western scholar quoted for testimony to the Faith's spread.

name: Jamál-i-Burújirdí
summary: A learned and once-trusted follower of Bahá'u'lláh (titled Ismu’lláhu’l-Jamál) who, after Bahá'u'lláh's passing, became Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí's ablest lieutenant and the chief Covenant-breaker in Persia; he died of a loathsome disease.
importance_reason: A leading Covenant-breaker whose defection is a taught cautionary episode; entrusted earlier with transferring the Báb's remains.
characterizations: Bahai-library: Jamál-i-Burújirdí rejected the covenant and rebelled against ‘Abdu’l-Bahá, serving as the leader of the Covenant-breakers in Iran and the chief representative of Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí there.

name: Ja‘far-Qulí Khán-i-Námdár
summary: A commander who, with Amír Arslán Khán, conducted operations against the Bábís with great cruelty during a Sháh-directed campaign; a single-mention military figure.
importance_reason: One DB mention as a cruel anti-Bábí commander.

name: Ja‘far-Qulí Ḵhán-i-Surḵh-Karri’í
summary: A name listed in DB with no surrounding detail; an incidental one-mention entry.
importance_reason: One bare DB mention with no descriptive context.

name: Ja‘far-i-Mázindarání
summary: A martyr of Mázindarán listed in DB's roster of the fallen; a one-mention martyr-list entry.
importance_reason: One-mention DB martyr-list entry.

name: Jean-Baptiste Louis Bourgeois
summary: French-Canadian architect who designed the Bahá'í House of Worship in Wilmette, Illinois, a selection confirmed by ‘Abdu’l-Bahá; remembered for the temple's distinctive design.
importance_reason: Three GPB mentions as the architect of the first American Mashriqu'l-Adhkár, a taught element of Bahá'í history.

name: Jesse
summary: Father of King David; invoked in GPB through Isaiah's prophecy of the 'Rod' and 'Branch' from the stem of Jesse, applied to Bahá'u'lláh's lineage.
importance_reason: Two GPB mentions used only in a genealogical-prophetic allusion.
